cataloging_subject.label: The Myroure of Oure Ladye [through the Sunday service]
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the U-M/General Corpus electronic selection from John Henry Blunt's 1873 EETS edition: the edition title page, Blunt's p. lx transcript of Aberdeen manuscript pages 1–2, and printed pp. 1–174 through the Sunday service. It is not the complete medieval Myroure, not the complete 1530 print or 1873 edition, and not a direct transcription of either surviving manuscript part. Monday through Saturday, feasts, Part III, notes, and index are outside this unit.

catalog_summary.title: The Myroure of Oure Ladye [through the Sunday service]
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Syon brother; Thomas Fishbourne has been proposed
catalog_summary.date: Composed after Syon's 1415 foundation, probably in the first half of the fifteenth century; first printed 1530; source edition 1873

titles.value: The Myroure of Oure Ladye [through the Sunday service]

assertion.notes: The terminus post quem is Syon's 1415 foundation. Modern discussion generally places the work in the first half of the fifteenth century, but exact authorship and date remain open.

notes: This is a composition range for the work, not a production date for either surviving manuscript part.

places.label: Syon Abbey
notes: The Bridgettine monastery associated with the work's composition, audience, office, and historical use.

assertion.notes: The work is securely associated with the Syon community, but a precise room or continuous site of composition is not documented.

notes: This records the work's Bridgettine institutional setting, not the later locations of the split manuscript parts or the 1873 publication place.

scope.description: Medieval treatise, instructions, translation, and commentary
Liturgical incipits, rubrics, quotations, and source phrases embedded in the Middle English prose
Blunt's title, headings, notes, introductory material, and supplied labels

form_statements.description: The selected medieval work is prose; embedded Latin liturgical phrases and incipits do not make the cataloged unit a mixed prose-and-verse work.

genre_statements.label: Vernacular religious translation
notes: The liturgical section translates and explains the Bridgettine office; it is not merely a word-for-word vernacular version.

scope.description: Instruction for the Bridgettine sisters' communal religious observance

summaries.value: The Myroure of Oure Ladye is an anonymous Middle English devotional and liturgical work made for the Bridgettine sisters of Syon Abbey. It explains why and how the divine office should be performed, instructs readers in attentive reading, and translates and comments on the community's Office of the Virgin, the Cantus sororum. CME00040 presents only the opening authorial material, Part I, reading instructions, and the Sunday office, framed by Blunt's title page and a short Aberdeen manuscript sample.

The electronic unit contains the 1873 title page; Blunt's p. lx transcript of legacy Aberdeen manuscript pages 1–2; two authorial prologues; a chapter table; Part I in twenty-four chapters on divine service; Part II reading instructions; the Sunday service through the colophon saying that Sunday is sufficient for this half; and a final editorial note to the reader. It omits the remaining weekday offices, feasts, Part III masses, and Blunt's later apparatus.

Although the electronic selection ends at the same Sunday boundary as the present Aberdeen half of the split manuscript, its main text is encoded from Blunt's 1873 edition, whose base is the 1530 Fawkes print. Only the p. lx sample directly transcribes Aberdeen manuscript pages 1–2.

extent.description: The XML has 8 top-level DIV1 containers—1 in FRONT and 7 in BODY—and 90 DIV2 containers: 2 prologues, 24 numbered Part I chapters, and 64 Sunday-service subdivisions. It also has 176 distinct PB references, 127 page/folio/leaf milestones, 111 P elements, 845 NOTE elements, and no LG or L verse hierarchy. The encoded print coverage is the edition title page, p. lx, and pp. 1–174; these are discontinuous in the complete scan. Counts describe XML/editorial structure rather than a canonical chapter count for the complete medieval work.

content_parts.label: Blunt edition title page
content_parts.summary: The bibliographic title page of the 1873 EETS source edition, encoded as editorial paratext rather than medieval work text.

content_parts.label: Blunt's Aberdeen manuscript sample
content_parts.summary: An editorially supplied transcript headed as pages 1 and 2 of the Aberdeen manuscript; it precedes the main printed text and is the only direct manuscript transcript established within CME00040.
notes: This sample should not be used to imply that the rest of CME00040 was transcribed from Aberdeen MS 134.

content_parts.label: Two authorial prologues
content_parts.summary: Two opening prologues frame the purpose and audience of the Myroure before Part I.

locators.value: 1–9; the chapter table begins later on shared p. 9

content_parts.label: Table of the twenty-four Part I chapters
content_parts.summary: A chapter list ending with the heading that begins Part I; it shares boundary pages with the second prologue and first chapter.

content_parts.label: Part I: On divine service
content_parts.summary: Twenty-four numbered chapters explain the purpose, value, and disciplined performance of divine service for the Syon sisters.

locators.value: 10–65; heading begins on shared p. 10 and Part II begins on shared p. 65

content_parts.label: Part II: Instruction for reading
content_parts.summary: A prose instruction on how the sisters should read and understand their service before the weekday offices begin.

locators.value: 65–71; section begins on shared p. 65 and Sunday heading begins on shared p. 71

content_parts.label: Sunday service
content_parts.summary: The Sunday portion of the Bridgettine Office of the Virgin, translated and explained in sixty-four XML subdivisions, ending with Tailour's colophon that Sunday is sufficient for this half of the Myroure.

locators.value: 71–174; heading begins on shared p. 71 and main text begins p. 72
145–248; Sunday heading begins on PDF p. 145 and main text begins p. 146

notes: Printed p. 174 is a textual boundary shared with the beginning of Monday in the complete edition; CME00040 stops after Sunday's closing statement.

content_parts.label: Editorial note to the reader
content_parts.summary: A brief final editorial note following the Sunday-service close on p. 174; it is paratext, not another medieval work section.

related_works.title: The Myroure of Oure Ladye (complete work)
notes: The complete work continues with Monday through Saturday, feasts, and Part III; the surviving historical Tailour codex is now split between two repositories.

related_works.title: Cantus sororum (Bridgettine Office of the Blessed Virgin Mary)
notes: The Myroure translates and explains this office, but its instructional and devotional prose extends beyond the translated liturgical text; a whole-unit translation_of claim would be too broad.

evidence.summary: Checked-in XML source; item identifiers, title page, prose hierarchy, page breaks, notes, and 2016 review statement.

evidence.citation: Local namespace-aware structural and printed-page inspection of CME00040.xml against Blunt's complete scan.
evidence.locator: FRONT/DIV1; BODY/DIV1, DIV2, HEAD, PB, MILESTONE, P, NOTE, CLOSER, and SIGNED
evidence.summary: Established 1 FRONT plus 7 BODY DIV1 and 90 DIV2 containers, 176 distinct PB references, the title/p. lx/pp. 1–174 selection, the two prologues, chapter table, 24 Part I chapters, reading section, 64 Sunday subdivisions, final reader note, prose form, and exact stopping point after Sunday.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00040 item record.
evidence.summary: Public item record and electronic text identifying Blunt's 1873 edition as the source publication.

evidence.citation: John Henry Blunt, ed., The Myroure of Oure Ladye (London: Early English Text Society, 1873), EETS Extra Series 19, complete Internet Archive scan.
evidence.locator: title page; p. lx; pp. 1–174 and complete continuation through p. 378
evidence.summary: Complete source edition establishing bibliographic title, editor, contents, selection boundary, prose organization, and material omitted from CME00040.

evidence.locator: pp. vii–xii and related introductory discussion
evidence.summary: Documents the 1415–1450 broad date argument, Syon setting, historical Gascoigne attribution, 1530 print, Durham H.II.24 base copy, and Aberdeen manuscript description.

evidence.citation: University of Aberdeen Special Collections, MS 134, The Myrroure of oure Lady.
evidence.summary: Current catalog identifies the late-fifteenth-century first half, Robert Tailour, English language, devotional contents for Syon's Bridgettine sisters, Sunday boundary, and Bodleian second half.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS. Rawl. C. 941, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries.
evidence.summary: Current catalog identifies the second part of Aberdeen MS 134, end-fifteenth-century Middle English paper codex, Syon and Elizabeth Monton provenance, and Robert Tailour inscription.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library, record 164108 for Richard Fawkes's 1530 Myroure, STC 17542; ESTC S111821.
evidence.summary: Authoritative bibliographic evidence for the early-print title, printer, exact completion date, extent, identifiers, and surviving Folger copies.

evidence.citation: Alexandra Verini, 'Sisters and Friends: The Medieval Nuns of Syon Abbey,' in Women's Friendship in Medieval Literature (Ohio State University Press, 2022), chapter 4.
evidence.summary: Recent scholarly treatment of the fifteenth-century translation and explication, Syon's nuns, possible Fishbourne authorship, and the split Tailour codex.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for CME00040.
evidence.summary: Provides entity-level relations distinguishing the repository artifact, U-M encoding, selected Blunt edition pages, 1530 Fawkes print, two present manuscript objects, catalog records, facsimile coverage, access routes, and rights findings.

open_questions.question: Which Syon brother authored, translated, and commented on the Myroure, and what date range does current textual scholarship support?
next_steps: Review Ann Hutchison's work and primary textual evidence for Thomas Fishbourne and other Syon candidates.
Retain anonymous responsibility and the conservative 1415–1450 range until competing proposals can be evaluated directly.
Keep Blunt's Gascoigne attribution explicitly historical and disputed.

open_questions.question: What complete modern work-part hierarchy should be used for the entire Myroure beyond the CME00040 selection?
next_steps: Map Monday through Saturday, feasts, and Part III from the complete Blunt edition and a modern critical account.
Distinguish medieval authorial divisions from Blunt's headings and U-M XML containers.
Do not project the 90 encoded DIV2 containers onto the omitted remainder of the work.

open_questions.question: What exact current folio ranges contain the Myroure in the complete present Aberdeen MS 134 and Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 941 objects, and where do Blunt's legacy manuscript pages 1–2 map?
next_steps: Request complete-object reproductions and current foliation from both repositories.
Map the relevant work portions within the full object deliveries rather than requesting only selected leaves.
Record bindings, blanks, adjacent matter, split boundary, and first/end work leaves separately from textual page ranges.

open_questions.question: Which manuscript or early-print features correspond to italics and other editorial interventions in the selected Blunt/U-M text?
next_steps: Establish Blunt's conventions from his edition rather than assuming all italics are one kind of abbreviation expansion.
Compare the 1530 base print and the direct Aberdeen p. lx sample against the encoded rendition.
Document U-M and General Corpus formatting transformations separately from Blunt's interventions.

notes: The preferred title's bracketed qualifier is supplied for this electronic selection and is not asserted as a medieval title form.
Composition, manuscript production, first printing, scholarly edition, U-M encoding, and repository representation are separate dates and layers.
Aberdeen MS 134 and Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 941 are two present physical objects from one historical codex; neither current shelfmark should be used as a synonym for the other.
The direct source for most CME00040 text is Blunt's edition of the 1530 print. Only the p. lx sample directly transcribes Aberdeen manuscript pages 1–2.
Exact facsimile availability, whole-object request scope, cache records, image rights, and publication or commercial-use constraints are maintained in the paired lineage manifest.
